artifacts.content:
Hi Jen,

I’m hiding in my office for ten minutes between walkthroughs, so you’re getting a brain dump.

You’d laugh if you saw my Outlook calendar right now – it’s basically color‑coded laps around Maple Ridge. Ever since the mild heart attack on June 14, 2021 at Cleveland Clinic Fairview Hospital, Dr. Elaine Morris has me on that 30‑minutes‑of‑moderate‑activity‑five‑days‑a‑week plan. The only way it actually happens as an assistant principal is breaking it into three 10‑minute walking blocks during the school day. I loop the halls between observations or walk the track with whatever kid needs to talk. It’s also why I’m so stuck on this “movement across the day” language in our wellness work instead of pretending one PE class or recess block magically fixes everything.

On the kid front, we’re juggling our own little case studies. Logan’s OT at Rainbow Children’s Rehab Center, Sarah McMillan, wrote in his October 2022 report that he needs a 5–10 minute heavy‑work or movement break every 45–60 minutes to stay regulated in 4th grade. I see the difference on nights he’s had those breaks versus days he’s white‑knuckled it. I’m trying to build something similar into classroom routines for other sensory kids so it isn’t all on individual teachers to remember.

And then there’s Mia. You remember her panic attack during 7th‑grade recess last March – Karen Diaz has her in weekly counseling now, and Dr. Patel at Maple Ridge Behavioral Health Clinic officially called it generalized anxiety. That’s what pushed me toward the quiet movement zone idea in the library courtyard – yoga mats, paced walking, low‑stimulation stuff so kids like her aren’t stuck choosing between a panic attack and sitting alone on a bench.

Anyway, that’s my glamorous life: walk, meeting, kid crisis, walk again. How’s Columbus treating you?

Love,
Becca

metadata.subject: Why I won’t shut up about movement

artifacts.content:
Hi Sarah,

I hope things are going well at Rainbow Children’s Rehab Center. I wanted to circle back now that we’ve lived with Logan’s updated plan for a while and also pick your brain in a slightly bigger‑picture way.

Your October 2022 report recommending a 5–10 minute heavy‑work or movement break every 45–60 minutes has been spot on for him. On days when his teacher actually builds those in, he comes home like a different kid – less wired, fewer meltdowns over homework. On days they get squeezed out for test prep, he’s bouncing off the walls and then crashes hard.

What I’m wrestling with (wearing my assistant principal hat at Maple Ridge Community School) is how to make that kind of structured break standard practice for students with similar sensory profiles, not just something Logan gets because his mom happens to work here and remembers to ask. I’m drafting some internal guidance for teachers about scheduled movement breaks tied to sensory needs – things like wall pushes, chair push‑ups, resistance bands, short hallway walks – and I’d love to be able to say, “These recommendations are consistent with what local OTs are already doing,” without naming any specific child.

Would you be comfortable if I referenced your October 2022 recommendations in a de‑identified way when I talk with our wellness/MTSS teams? I won’t use Logan’s name or Rainbow’s name in anything that goes beyond our internal planning, but it helps people to know this isn’t just me making it up because I used to be a PE teacher.

Also, practical question: are there 2–3 “heavy work” activities you’ve seen work well in regular classrooms with limited space and no extra equipment? Teachers are more willing to try things if I can hand them a short, concrete list.

Thanks for everything you’ve done for Logan.

Best,
Rebecca

metadata.subject: Logan’s movement breaks & broader classroom practice

artifacts.content:
Personal reflections – wellness/PE work (for me only)

1. Mia – quiet movement + PE anxiety

I still can’t stop thinking about Mia’s panic attack during 7th‑grade recess back in March 2023. Watching my own kid hyperventilate on the bench while the rest of the playground kept screaming was awful. Karen Diaz has been seeing her weekly, and the generalized anxiety diagnosis from Dr. Patel at Maple Ridge Behavioral Health Clinic finally gave it a name, but the environment is still loud and chaotic. That’s why the “quiet movement” courtyard matters so much to me – yoga, stretching, paced walking, pedometers – a way to meet movement expectations without throwing kids like her into sensory overload.

Related: her mile‑run episode in November 2023 in 8th‑grade PE. Full‑on panic in the Maple Ridge gym. I pushed Lauren Diaz and Kevin Morris to write a protocol so any student with a documented anxiety disorder can do a 20‑minute brisk walk around the back field instead of a timed mile, with access to the small conference room next to the gym if they start to spiral. Need to remember: keep Mia’s name out of anything formal, but don’t lose the urgency this came from.

2. My own body

April 2023 ACL repair at Maple Ridge Orthopedic Center (Dr. Harris) + months of PT = new appreciation for kids quietly dealing with pain. It’s why I’m so focused on low‑impact options in PE (yoga, stretching) and on not using activity as punishment.

Note to self: all of this is personal AND systemic. That’s okay.

metadata.title: Reflection – why the “quiet movement” stuff matters so much

artifacts.content:
Hi Mark,

Quick update on a few things that have been on my mind before our next leadership meeting.

1) Unstructured time + behavior

After the September 18, 2025 vaping incident with Marcus Hill behind the bleachers during after‑school intramural basketball in the gym, I started tagging office referrals that happen during recess, PE, and intramurals. The pattern is pretty clear: when kids don’t have enough structure or don’t see those spaces as “for them,” we get more problems. I’d like to weave a goal into our wellness work to pair Fit Friday with 10–15 minutes of SEL and basic substance‑use prevention at least twice per quarter. I’ve already talked with Erin Walters and Officer James Nolan; both are willing to co‑plan short, practical segments that don’t feel like another lecture.

2) Restorative, movement‑based responses

You probably remember the 5th‑grader last November 2023 when I initially wrote up a two‑day in‑school suspension for repeated playground aggression. After consulting with Kevin Liu and reviewing his behavior plan/trauma history, we shifted to a daily 15‑minute supervised walking and movement break around the track plus a self‑regulation routine in the gym. His referrals dropped, and teachers reported fewer blow‑ups. I think we should capture that approach explicitly as an option in our discipline toolkit.

3) Access and equity

Transportation is still a huge barrier. Since October 2024 I’ve been quietly covering Uber rides (usually $18–$24) for 2–3 girls from Oak Street Apartments so they can stay for girls’ running club, then submitting a spreadsheet to PTO treasurer Melissa Grant for reimbursement. It’s not sustainable, and I’m uncomfortable relying on rideshare for students. Long term, I’d like us to advocate for a district‑funded late bus by August 2026.

Happy to talk through any of this in more detail.

Best,
Rebecca

metadata.subject: Behavior during activity time + access issues

artifacts.content:
Event: After‑school walk + Zumba (BP/weight goal)

Date: Recurring – Mondays, Wednesdays, Thursdays
Time: 3:30–4:30 p.m.
Location: Maple Ridge Community School track / gym
Attendees: Rebecca (optional invite to any staff who want to join)

Notes:

Started this routine after my April 3, 2024 appointment with Dr. Kimberly Larson at the Cleveland Clinic cardiology clinic. She flagged my blood pressure and cholesterol as “borderline high” and started me on lisinopril. Personal goal: walk the Maple Ridge track for 30 minutes after dismissal three days a week and join the staff Zumba session in the gym on Thursdays.

Target: lose 15–20 pounds by December 2025 and, with Dr. Larson’s okay, hopefully reduce my reliance on the lisinopril. Bigger picture, I want to be visibly active on campus so it feels normal for adults to move, not just kids in PE.

To remember:
- Bring sneakers to school on these days (no more trying to walk in dress shoes).
- If it’s raining or icy, swap track walk for indoor hallway laps.
- Invite Erin, Dana, and any other staff who mention wanting to get more active – keep it low‑pressure, no weigh‑ins or diet talk.
- On Thursdays, Zumba class in the gym starts at 3:45; aim to wrap up dismissal by 3:30 so I’m not sprinting in late.

Also tie‑in: this helps me stay honest when I talk with students about heart‑health and stress‑management in PE. If I’m asking them to build movement into their day, I need to be doing the same.

metadata.title: Walk track after school (BP/weight goal)
metadata.location: Maple Ridge Community School track / gym

artifacts.content:
Hi Andrew,

Thanks again for being so flexible with Mia this winter. I know it’s an extra layer of planning on top of everything else you’re juggling in PE.

Just to have everything in one place: after her ACL sprain during 8th‑grade basketball practice in the Maple Ridge gym on January 22, 2025, her orthopedist at MetroHealth, Dr. Jason Patel, recommended at least 12 weeks of modified activity. He was clear that she should stay moving, just keep it low‑impact and avoid cutting/jumping.

What you’ve been doing – letting her walk laps, use light resistance bands, and help with peer coaching on days when the main activity is higher impact – has made a huge difference in her mood. She’s not coming home feeling like “the injured kid stuck on the bleachers,” which, given everything with her anxiety over the past couple of years, is a big win.

Selfishly, watching you handle this so thoughtfully has me thinking about how we can turn Mia’s situation into a template for other students. Every year we have at least a handful of kids coming back from ACL repairs, fractures, or other injuries, and we end up reinventing the wheel each time. I’d love to sit down before the start of the 2025–26 school year and sketch out a simple return‑to‑activity protocol: sample modifications by injury type, communication templates for families, and a quick check‑in form for the nurse/counselor when kids are repeatedly medically excused.

If you’re open to it, maybe we could grab 30 minutes in August to draft something together with Kevin. Having it come from the PE team, not just from me, would carry more weight.

Thanks again for taking such good care of her.

Rebecca

metadata.subject: Mia’s ACL injury – PE modifications & bigger picture

artifacts.content:
Planning notes – PE & wellness priorities (2024–2026)

1) Trauma‑informed movement plans

Lily Thompson: after the May 6, 2025 re‑entry meeting with her, mom, and Dr. Anjali Mehta at Maple Ridge Behavioral Health Center, we agreed she can spend the first 10 minutes of PE walking laps on the back field with Dana Ruiz before joining loud, competitive games in the main gym to manage PTSD‑related panic. Goal: expand similar individualized plans to 5–6 students by spring 2026.

5th‑grader (Nov 2023): initially wrote a two‑day ISS for repeated playground aggression. After consulting with Kevin Liu and reviewing behavior plan/Children’s Services history, we switched to a daily 15‑minute supervised walking/movement break on the track plus a self‑regulation routine in the gym. Referrals decreased. Need to formalize restorative, movement‑based options.

2) Safety + activity design

February 2024 – Aiden’s wrist fracture during full‑court basketball in the gym while several classmates stood idle on the baseline. Pushed us toward small‑sided, half‑court games for 6–8 PE with a written expectation that no more than two students wait on the sideline for longer than two minutes.

3) Access + equity

Transportation: since October 2024, have been covering Uber rides ($18–$24) for 2–3 girls from Oak Street Apartments to get home after girls’ running club; PTO treasurer Melissa Grant reimburses from PTO funds. Long‑term goal: district‑funded late bus by August 2026.

4) Data + subgroups

August 2024 analysis of PACER/curl‑up data showed lower scores for students on free/reduced lunch and those from Maple Ridge Estates. Personal sting remembering when Daniel’s hours at Lakeview Engineering were cut in 2020 and we briefly qualified for SNAP. Goal: by June 2026, track/report fitness trends by subgroup and use data to target supports.

From: Rebecca Caldwell <rcaldwell@mrcs.k12.oh.us>
To: Wellness/PE Committee
Date: 2024-11-06
Subject: Draft PE goals – stress management, inclusive options, and medical excuses

Hi team,

Ahead of next week’s meeting, I wanted to capture a few concrete PE goals I’d like us to consider for Maple Ridge Community School.

1) **Heart-health & stress-management mini-unit**
I’d like us to formalize a goal that all students complete a basic heart-health and stress-management mini-unit by May 2025. This would include simple breathing and movement routines that can be used in class or at home. This is personal for me: after my daughter Mia’s anxiety diagnosis at Maple Ridge Pediatric Associates in October 2022 (with Dr. Patel), I saw how much a short, structured movement/breathing routine helped her, even before she was fully connected with counseling supports. I’d like our students to have similar tools.

2) **Inclusive fitness for autism/ADHD**
I’m also proposing that by Fall 2025 we add a specific PE goal around inclusive fitness options for students with autism and ADHD—quiet equipment zones, visual schedules, and clear “opt-in” spaces. This is heavily influenced by what I’ve learned from my son Logan’s occupational therapy at Riverside Children’s Therapy since January 2021 and how overwhelmed he gets in our own Maple Ridge Community School gym during family nights.

3) **Protocol for frequent medical excuses**
Finally, I’d like us to build a documented plan for students who are frequently medically excused from activity, including a check-in protocol with the school nurse (e.g., Mrs. Delgado) and counselor. Last spring (April–May 2024) we had a 7th-grade student, “A.,” with repeated MetroHealth Maple Ridge Clinic notes for vague pain; Mom later disclosed it was tied to an ongoing custody dispute and stress at home rather than a true physical limitation.

Let’s use these as a starting point for discussion.

Rebecca
Rebecca Anne Caldwell, Assistant Principal

metadata.subject: Draft PE goals – stress management, inclusive options, and medical excuses

artifacts.content:
From: Rebecca Caldwell <rcaldwell@mrcs.k12.oh.us>
To: Logan IEP Team (H. Blake, A. Schultz, M. Rivera, J. Owens)
Date: 2024-03-18
Subject: Logan – integrating OT goals into PE

Hi all,

Following up on Logan’s IEP goal update: after his occupational therapist at Maple Ridge Pediatric Therapy, Ms. Hannah Blake, revised his fine-motor and balance goals earlier this month (March 2024), I asked our 4th-grade PE teacher, Mr. Aaron Schultz, to explicitly incorporate a written balance-and-core objective into Logan’s PE plan.

Aaron and I discussed carving out 10-minute weekly sessions on the small side court of the Maple Ridge Community School gym so he can work on those targeted skills in a slightly quieter, more structured space. The idea is that Aaron will document Logan’s progress during those 10-minute weekly sessions so we have concrete data to bring to the IEP review meeting in May 2024.

Hannah, if you’re comfortable, it would be helpful to have a brief summary of the updated OT goals in language Aaron can easily translate into PE activities (e.g., specific balance tasks, core-stability movements). Aaron, please let us know what tools or equipment you might need; I can try to cover small items out of our building wellness budget.

My hope is that this becomes a small model for how we integrate OT and PE more broadly for students who need adapted PE support, not just Logan.

Thank you all for your collaboration on this.

Best,
Rebecca
Rebecca Anne Caldwell
Assistant Principal, Maple Ridge Community School

metadata.recipient: Logan IEP Team (H. Blake, A. Schultz, M. Rivera, J. Owens)
metadata.subject: Logan – integrating OT goals into PE

artifacts.content:
From: Rebecca Caldwell <rebecca.caldwell@gmail.com>
To: Jennifer Pierce <jennifer.pierce@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-09-08
Subject: PE ideas I’m stealing from our own family

Hey Jen,

I was laughing today thinking about how much of my “professional innovation” is just formalizing what we’ve been doing as a family.

Since August 2023 I’ve been informally piloting our new PE activity log with Mia and Logan at home. I ask them to jot down everyday things like our 30-minute evening walks around Maple Ridge Park and the Saturday bike rides to Mom and Dad’s place (Carolyn and Thomas’s house is right at the 2‑mile mark, which makes it an easy target). I’ve been having them add short reflections on stress and sleep—how they feel before/after, whether it helps with mood swings or getting off their screens.

Their notes about feeling less stressed and actually sleeping better after those walks and rides are pure gold. I’m lifting their language almost verbatim to shape the SEL pieces we’re embedding into PE lessons about how regular movement can help with mood and screen-time overuse.

It also ties back to why I’m so focused on this heart-health and stress-management stuff at school. After Mia’s anxiety diagnosis at Maple Ridge Pediatric Associates in October 2022 and that initial counseling referral discussion with Dr. Patel, it became crystal clear how much structured movement can help kids who are struggling emotionally but aren’t yet plugged into formal supports.

Anyway, tell Columbus I said hi. Come up for a Maple Ridge Park walk soon.

Love,
Becca

metadata.subject: PE ideas I’m stealing from our own family
